title: Context Rot, Revisited in Depth
concept: Why model accuracy degrades as irrelevant or excess context accumulates, even within the stated context limit.

exercises.prompt: Since this is the first lesson of a new unit, let's start with a warm-up question: if you gave a friend a 50-page document and asked them one simple question buried on page 30, what would likely happen?

options.text: They'd answer perfectly every time, no matter how much filler text surrounds the answer
They might struggle or miss details, especially if most of the pages are irrelevant clutter
They'd refuse to read anything longer than one page

exercises.explain: Just like a person skimming a huge pile of pages, an AI model can lose track of important details when they're buried in a lot of irrelevant text. This is the core idea behind 'context rot,' which we'll dig into today.

cards.title: More Isn't Always Better
cards.body: You'd think giving an AI MORE information would always help it answer better. But surprisingly, stuffing a prompt with tons of extra text can actually make the AI's answers worse, even if the important fact is technically still in there somewhere.

cards.title: What Is Context Rot?
cards.body: Context rot is when an AI model's accuracy drops as its input (the 'context') fills up with irrelevant or excessive text, even though the total length is still within what the model technically allows. The model doesn't run out of room, it just gets worse at using what's there.

cards.title: The Overstuffed Backpack
cards.body: Imagine packing a backpack with your one important textbook plus 40 random items you don't need. The book is still in there, but now you have to dig through junk to find it, and you might grab the wrong thing by mistake. A cluttered prompt works the same way for an AI.

cards.title: A Real Example
cards.body: Ask an AI a question with just the 3 relevant sentences, and it nails the answer. Paste those same 3 sentences into a 5,000-word document full of unrelated facts, and the AI may suddenly give a vague or wrong answer, even though nothing important was removed.

cards.title: Myth: Context Limit Is the Only Problem
cards.body: Many people think accuracy only drops when you hit the model's maximum context size. That's false. Accuracy can degrade well BEFORE the limit is reached, simply because there's too much noise for the model to sort through effectively.

cards.title: Lean Prompt vs. Bloated Prompt
cards.body: Same core fact, very different outcomes depending on what surrounds it.

left.text: Just the relevant facts. The model finds the answer quickly and confidently.

right.text: Same facts buried in irrelevant text. The model gets distracted and may miss or misstate the answer.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best describes context rot?

options.text: The AI's memory chip physically wears out over time
Accuracy can decline as irrelevant or excess context builds up, even within the allowed limit
The AI always performs best with the maximum possible context length
Context rot only happens after the context limit is exceeded

exercises.explain: Context rot describes a real drop in accuracy that happens because of clutter and irrelevant information, not because the model physically broke or ran out of space. It can happen well within the stated limit.

exercises.prompt: True or False: An AI model can lose accuracy on a task even if it hasn't reached its maximum context length.
exercises.explain: True! Context rot can happen far before the limit is hit. It's about the ratio of useful-to-irrelevant information, not just raw space.

exercises.sentence: Context rot happens when a model's accuracy drops due to irrelevant or excess text, even within its stated context limit.
exercises.explain: The key cause of context rot is irrelevant (or excessive) text cluttering the model's input, making it harder to focus on what matters.

exercises.prompt: One of these three explanations of context rot was written by an AI trying to sound smart but is actually misleading. Spot it.

options.text: Context rot is when too much irrelevant text makes it harder for a model to locate and use the important details, lowering accuracy.
Context rot occurs because the model's neural pathways physically decay after processing large amounts of text, similar to metal rusting.
Context rot means accuracy can drop even before the context limit is reached, simply due to clutter and noise in the input.

exercises.explain: Option B is the misleading one. It borrows a science-sounding metaphor ('neural pathways physically decay,' 'rusting') that sounds convincing but is not how models work. There's no physical decay, the model just struggles to filter signal from noise in a given context window.

exercises.prompt: A team feeds their AI a support ticket with the customer's key issue in one sentence, surrounded by 2,000 words of unrelated chat logs. The AI gives a vague, incorrect summary of the issue. What is the MOST likely explanation, and what does it tell us about context rot?

options.text: The ticket exceeded the model's maximum context limit, causing a hard failure
The model ran out of computing power partway through reading the ticket
The irrelevant chat logs diluted the important sentence, making it harder for the model to locate and prioritize the real issue, showing that clutter alone can hurt accuracy within the limit
The AI intentionally ignores customer issues to save processing time

exercises.explain: This is a textbook case of context rot: the input was almost certainly within the model's context limit, so the drop in quality came from the flood of irrelevant text drowning out the one important sentence. This shows that just being 'under the limit' doesn't guarantee good performance, the ratio of relevant-to-irrelevant content matters a lot. Keeping prompts lean and focused is one of the best ways engineers can fight context rot.
